reforms.description: The Digital Switzerland Strategy aims to leverage digital transformation to enhance innovation, promote prosperity, and ensure equal opportunities while focusing on transparency and sustainable development. It provides a framework for government policy and action by other actors to support the digital transformation in Switzerland.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The strategy aims to enhance innovation and prosperity through digital transformation, which is expected to raise productivity and efficiency.
reforms.status_evidence: The strategy has been officially implemented as of 2019.
reforms.importance_rationale: The strategy represents a structural change in how the government approaches digital transformation and innovation.
reforms.source_quote: The government’s 'Digital Switzerland' strategy recognises Switzerland’s strong starting point for the digital transformation and aims to provide a framework for government policy as well as action by other actors.

reforms.description: The Swiss authorities have implemented a three-pillar approach to fintech regulation, which includes specific regulatory adjustments, the creation of a regulatory sandbox, and the introduction of FinTech licenses to facilitate the entry and operation of fintech firms.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The regulatory adjustments and sandbox are designed to foster innovation and growth in the fintech sector, which can enhance economic activity.
reforms.status_evidence: The three-pillar approach has been officially implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant overhaul of the regulatory framework for fintech, aimed at promoting innovation in the sector.
reforms.source_quote: A three-pillar approach to fintech regulation was completed in early 2019.

reforms.description: The Swiss government plans to introduce tax allowances for R&D expenditure of 120-150% to encourage investment by domestic firms, with specific plans to broaden R&D activity to younger and smaller firms in 17 cantons.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The introduction of tax allowances is expected to incentivize R&D investment, thereby enhancing innovation and productivity among firms.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has announced plans to introduce these tax allowances.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant change in the fiscal policy towards R&D, aimed at stimulating innovation.
reforms.source_quote: Plans to introduce R&D tax allowances in 17 cantons may also help broaden R&D activity to younger and smaller firms.

reforms.description: The authorities have proposed reducing red tape associated with tariffs and with registering a business to promote a dynamic business environment that can spur the adoption of digital technologies.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Reducing red tape is expected to facilitate business operations and encourage the adoption of innovative technologies.
reforms.status_evidence: The proposal has been announced but not yet implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: While significant, this reform is not as comprehensive as others and focuses on specific regulatory adjustments.
reforms.source_quote: The authorities have proposed reducing red tape associated with tariffs and with registering a business; these plans should be implemented.

reforms.description: In 2018, the Swiss government launched a programme subsidising employer-provided training in basic skills, which reached 1,500 participants in its first year. New measures announced in 2019 aim to expand access to training for older jobseekers and include free career guidance and skills assessment.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The programme aims to enhance the skills of the workforce, which is essential for innovation and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The programme has been officially implemented since 2018.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant investment in human capital aimed at improving workforce skills.
reforms.source_quote: In 2018 the Swiss government launched a programme subsidising employer-provided training in basic skills, including IT, which reached 1,500 participants in the first year.
